coredata.dc:title: Structures and magnetic properties of Nd–Fe–Ti alloys produced by melt-spinning technique
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Saito, Tetsuji
Wang, Wen Quan
Kamagata, Yasushi
coredata.dc:description: Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) alloys produced by the melt-spinning technique consisted of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and amorphous phases. The amount of amorphous phase in the melt-spun ribbons increased as the Ti content increased. Annealing of the melt-spun ribbons resulted in the formation of the single Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. The saturation magnetization of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase was found to be 120Am2 kg−1 at room temperature.

Intermetallic compounds
Amorphous materials
Melt-spinning
Magnetic properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5, (b) Nd3Fe27.3Ti1.7 and (c) Nd3Fe27Ti2 melt-spun ribbons.
Thermomagnetic curves of (a) Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5, (b) Nd3Fe27.3Ti1.7 and (c) Nd3Fe27Ti2 melt-spun ribbons.
XRD patterns of Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbons annealed at (a) 673K, (b) 773K, (c) 873K, (d) 973K, (e) 1073K and (f) 1173K for 1h.
Dependence of the saturation magnetization and coercivity of Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbons on the annealing temperature.

article.ce:title: Structures and magnetic properties of Nd–Fe–Ti alloys produced by melt-spinning technique

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Science and Engineering, Chiba Institute of Technology, 2-17-1 Tsudanuma, Narashino, Chiba 275-0016, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jilin University, JieFang Road 119, Changchun 130023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) alloys produced by the melt-spinning technique consisted of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and amorphous phases. The amount of amorphous phase in the melt-spun ribbons increased as the Ti content increased. Annealing of the melt-spun ribbons resulted in the formation of the single Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. The saturation magnetization of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase was found to be 120Am2 kg−1 at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: Since it was found that Nd–Fe–B permanent magnets exhibit excellent hard magnetic properties, research and development of new permanent magnetic materials has largely concentrated on rare earth-containing alloys [1,2]. One of the rare earth compounds for the new permanent magnetic materials is the R3(Fe,M)29 (R: rare earth and M: transition metal) phase [3]. This R3(Fe,M)29 phase is described as an alternate stacking of the R(Fe,M)12 phase (tetragonal ThMn12-type structure) and the R2(Fe,M)17 phase (rhombohedral Th2Zn17-type structure), which are very common intermetallic compounds in the field of rare earth permanent magnets. The complexity of the structural arrangement of the R3(Fe,M)29 phase has given rise to the contradictory reports concerning the magnetocrystalline anisotropy of the R3(Fe,M)29 phase [4,5]. Since the R3(Fe,M)29 phase is an intermediate phase between the ThMn12- and Th2Zn17-type structures, production of the single R3(Fe,M)29 phase is difficult and time consuming. For example, the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase is usually produced by annealing of Nd–Fe–Ti alloy ingots at a high temperature of 1373K for a long period of 72–130h [6,7]. The annealed Nd–Fe–Ti alloy still contains a small amount of the α-Fe phase together with the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase in most cases [3,8]. It has been reported that Nd–Fe–B alloys with a peritectic Nd2Fe14B phase, but without any primary α-Fe phase, can be directly obtained by annealing of amorphous Nd–Fe–B alloys produced by the melt-spinning technique [9,10]. The single Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase may be obtained by a short annealing of amorphous Nd–Fe–Ti alloys produced by the melt-spinning technique. In this study, the amorphous Nd–Fe–Ti alloys were produced by the melt-spinning technique and the structure and magnetic properties of the amorphous Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons and those annealed specimens were investigated.

Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) alloys were prepared by arc melting in an argon atmosphere. Small quantities of the alloy ingots were placed in a quartz crucible with an orifice of 0.6mm in diameter at the bottom. The alloy ingots were induction-melted in an argon atmosphere and then ejected through the orifice under argon pressure onto a chromium-plated copper wheel rotating at a surface velocity of 50ms−1. The resultant melt-spun ribbons were obtained as fragmented pieces. Typical dimensions of the ribbon fragments were 10μm in thickness by 1mm wide and 100mm long. Heat treatments of the melt-spun ribbons were then carried out in an argon atmosphere at temperatures between 673 and 1173K for 1h. The specimens were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation. The magnetization of the powders was examined at a heating rate of 0.16Ks−1 in a vacuum using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at an applied field of 40kAm−1. The hysteresis loops of the specimens were measured at room temperature by VSM under a maximum applied field of 2.0MAm−1.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons. The XRD patterns of the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon shows diffraction peaks of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. The existence of a fairly broad halo-like peak at 2θ =30–50° is also noted in the XRD pattern. The broad halo-like peak is a characteristic of an amorphous material. This indicates that the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon contains the amorphous phase together with the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. The high cooling rate attained by melt-spinning results in the formation of the amorphous phase, as has also been reported for Nd–Fe–B melt-spun ribbons [11]. The diffraction peaks of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase become smaller and broader as the Ti content of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbon increases. This indicates that the amount of amorphous phase in the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbon increases with increasing Ti content. A further increase in the Ti content of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbon may lead to fully amorphous Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbon. However, such an increase in the Ti content would deteriorate the formation of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase, which is usually obtained in Nd–Fe–Ti alloys with a Ti content of 4–5at% [8]. Therefore, we selected Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons with Ti contents ranging from Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 (4.69at% Ti) to Nd3Fe27Ti2 (6.25at% Ti) for investigation in our experiments.

As far as we know, there have been no previous reports on the formation of the amorphous phase in Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons. We therefore studied the magnetization of amorphous Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons. Fig. 2 shows the thermomagnetic curves of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons. The thermomagnetic curve of the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon shows two magnetic transitions at about 350 and 450K. The higher magnetic transition is the Curie temperature of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. According to the results of the XRD studies, the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon consisted of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase together with the amorphous phase. Thus, the lower magnetic transition is considered to be the Curie temperature of the amorphous phase. Virtually, the same thermomagnetic curves are obtained from the Nd3Fe27.3Ti1.7 and Nd3Fe27Ti2 melt-spun ribbons. A detailed inspection of the thermomagnetic curves reveals that the amount of Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase becomes smaller as the Ti content of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbon increases. This is consistent with the results of the XRD studies. The increase in magnetization at around 700K is believed to be due to crystallization from the amorphous Nd–Fe–Ti phase. The crystallized phase shows a magnetic transition at around 1050K, which corresponds to the Curie temperature of the α-Fe phase. XRD studies of the sample heated to 1073K during the thermomagnetic measurements confirmed that the sample consisted of the α-Fe phase together with the Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 and Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phases. This indicates that isochronal annealing results in the formation of the α-Fe and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases from the mixture of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and amorphous phases.

The single Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase has been obtained by isothermal annealing of the Nd–Fe–Ti alloy ingots at a high temperature of 1373K for a long period of 72–130h [6,7]. Thus, isothermal annealing of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons was carried out to form melt-spun ribbons with the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbons annealed at temperatures between 673 and 1173K for 1h. The XRD pattern of the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 673K shows small and somewhat broad diffraction peaks of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and α-Fe phases. However, the halo-like peak is evident in the XRD pattern. This indicates that the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 673K consists of an amorphous phase together with small amounts of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and α-Fe phases. The halo-like peak is still noticed in the XRD pattern of the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 773K. Besides the diffraction peaks of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and α-Fe phases, diffraction peaks of the Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phase are also found in the XRD pattern. Because of the primary crystallization of the α-Fe phase from the amorphous phase, the residual amorphous phase was enriched in Nd and Ti. This is why the Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phase, which is poorer in iron content than Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase, crystallized from the iron-poor amorphous phase. Evaluation of the respective amounts of Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases is difficult due to the overlapping diffraction peaks. However, the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase forms from the α-Fe and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases at a higher annealing temperature. Thus, the amount of Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phase can be estimated from the amount of α-Fe phase in the melt-spun ribbon. The diffraction peak of the α-Fe phase becomes larger until the amorphous phase diminishes at 873K. A further increase in the annealing temperature results in an increase in the amount of Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase and in a significant decrease in the amount of α-Fe phase. No noticeable diffraction peak of the α-Fe phase is found in the XRD pattern of the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 1173K. Although crystallization of the iron-rich Nd(Fe,Ti)12 phase from the iron-poor amorphous phase is difficult, the Nd(Fe,Ti)12 phase may form during high-temperature annealing. Identification of the diffraction peaks of the Nd(Fe,Ti)12 phase is very difficult, however, because most of the diffraction peaks overlap those of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phases. No clear non-overlapping peak of the Nd(Fe,Ti)12 phase was found in the XRD pattern, suggesting that the amount of the Nd(Fe,Ti)12 phase is negligible. Virtually, the same XRD patterns were obtained from the annealed Nd3Fe27.3Ti1.7 and Nd3Fe27Ti2 melt-spun ribbons. This indicates that the small difference in the Ti content of the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons, that is, the small difference in the amount of amorphous phase in the Nd–Fe–Ti melt-spun ribbons, does not alter the formation of the crystalline phase from the amorphous structure. The thermomagnetic curve of the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon annealed at 1173K showed a single magnetic transition at 450K, confirming that the annealed Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon consists of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase.

The magnetic properties of the annealed Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) melt-spun ribbons were measured. Similar trends, that is, virtually the same saturation magnetization and coercivity values, were observed in the annealed Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) melt-spun ribbons. Thus, only the magnetic properties of the annealed Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbons are shown here. Fig. 4 shows the dependence of the saturation magnetization and coercivity on the annealing temperature of the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbons. The coercivity of the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 673K remains as low as that of the as-melt-spun ribbon, but increases to a peak value of 50kAm−1 and then decreases as the annealing temperature increases. The highest coercivity of 50kAm−1 is seen at 873K, where the melt-spun ribbon consists of a mixture of the α-Fe, Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases. Since the α-Fe and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases are soft magnetic phases, the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase is believed to be the origin of the coercivity. This would confirm that the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase is the hard magnetic phase. The melt-spun ribbon with the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase, obtained by annealing at 1173K, shows a lower coercivity than that with a mixture of the α-Fe, Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases, where the crystallite size of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase is relatively small. This implies that the coercivity of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase is dependent on the crystallite size, as is the case for the hard magnetic Nd2Fe14B phase of Nd–Fe–B alloys [12]. The saturation magnetization increases from 90Am2 kg−1 at 673K to 180Am2 kg−1at 873K and then decreases as the annealing temperature increases further to a value of 120Am2 kg−1at 1173K. According to the results of the XRD studies, the existence of the α-Fe phase with a high saturation magnetization gives rise to the observed higher saturation magnetization of the melt-spun ribbons annealed at 773 and 873K. The saturation magnetization of the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 1173K, which consists of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase, is found to be 120Am2 kg−1. This value is slightly smaller than the previously reported value of 139.6Am2 kg−1 [5]. The annealed Nd3Fe27.3Ti1.7 and Nd3Fe27.0Ti2.0 melt-spun ribbons with the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase also exhibit the same saturation magnetization as the Nd3Fe27.5Ti1.5 melt-spun ribbon. This confirms that the saturation magnetization of the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase is 120Am2 kg−1 at room temperature.

Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) alloys with the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 and amorphous phases were produced by the melt-spinning technique. XRD studies and magnetic measurements revealed that the amount of amorphous phase in the melt-spun ribbons increased as the Ti content increased. The Curie temperature of the amorphous phase was found to be 350K. Although the melt-spun ribbon annealed at relatively lower temperatures contained some α-Fe and Nd2(Fe,Ti)17 phases besides the Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase, the melt-spun ribbon annealed at 1173K for 1h consisted of the single Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase. Regardless of their Ti content, the saturation magnetization of the annealed melt-spun Nd3Fe29−x Ti x (x =1.5–2.0) melt-spun ribbons with the single Nd3(Fe,Ti)29 phase was 120Am2 kg−1 at room temperature.
